📚 Additional Documentation

Deep Research Bioreason

(Akt1-deep-research-bioreason.md)

BioReason Chat Export

Exported on March 22, 2026 at 03:11 AM

Organism: Mus musculus

Sequence:

MNDVAIVKEGWLHKRGEYIKTWRPRYFLLKNDGTFIGYKERPQDVDQRESPLNNFSVAQCQLMKTERPRPNTFIIRCLQWTTVIERTFHVETPEEREEWATAIQTVADGLKRQEEETMDFRSGSPSDNSGAEEMEVSLAKPKHRVTMNEFEYLKLLGKGTFGKVILVKEKATGRYYAMKILKKEVIVAKDEVAHTLTENRVLQNSRHPFLTALKYSFQTHDRLCFVMEYANGGELFFHLSRERVFSEDRARFYGAEIVSALDYLHSEKNVVYRDLKLENLMLDKDGHIKITDFGLCKEGIKDGATMKTFCGTPEYLAPEVLEDNDYGRAVDWWGLGVVMYEMMCGRLPFYNQDHEKLFELILMEEIRFPRTLGPEAKSLLSGLLKKDPTQRLGGGSEDAKEIMQHRFFANIVWQDVYEKKLSPPFKPQVTSETDTRYFDEEFTAQMITITPPDQDDSMECVDSERRPHFPQFSYSASGTA

Thinking Trace

I begin with the InterPro architecture. The N-terminus (residues 1–123) carries IPR011993 (PH-like domain superfamily), within which three pleckstrin-homology annotations converge: IPR039026 (Protein Kinase B, pleckstrin homology domain, residues 4–111), IPR001849 (Pleckstrin homology domain, residues 5–110), and the AKT/kinase B PH signature IPR034676 (Protein kinase B alpha, catalytic domain, residues 124–479). This tandem clearly establishes a canonical PH module that binds phosphoinositide headgroups and thereby recruits the kinase to membrane-proximal signaling sites. Immediately downstream lies the bilobal catalytic core: IPR034676 overlaps and transitions into the kinase region marked by IPR011009 (Protein kinase-like domain superfamily, residues 145–459) and IPR000719 (Protein kinase domain, residues 150–408). The active chemistry is specified by IPR017441 (Protein kinase, ATP binding site, residues 156–189) and IPR008271 (Serine/threonine-protein kinase, active site, residues 270–282), which together define an ATP-dependent serine/threonine phosphotransferase mechanism. The C-terminus is characteristic of AGC kinases: IPR000961 (AGC-kinase, C-terminal, residues 409–480) and IPR017892 (Protein kinase, C-terminal, residues 429–474) encode the regulatory tail that stabilizes the active conformation and integrates activation-loop and hydrophobic motif phosphorylation.

This ordered layout—membrane-targeting PH domain followed by an AGC-type catalytic core and regulatory tail—causes ATP-dependent serine/threonine kinase activity. The PH domain dictates lipid-dependent localization and substrate selection, while the kinase core and AGC tail execute phosphorylation of protein substrates; together these features support GO:0004674 protein serine/threonine kinase activity and the broader GO:0004672 protein kinase activity. The presence of a high-affinity ATP-binding pocket (residues 156–189) and an AGC activation segment (residues 270–282) ensures robust catalytic function.

From this molecular function, the biological process follows. PH-directed recruitment to phosphoinositide-enriched membranes positions the enzyme downstream of PI3K signaling. There, it phosphorylates targets that regulate metabolism, growth, and survival. This places the protein squarely in phosphatidylinositol-mediated signaling and explains its role in broader intracellular signaling cascades. Thus, the kinase architecture and membrane targeting logically drive GO:0006468 protein phosphorylation and implicate processes such as PI3K signaling and its downstream pathways.

The cellular component is inferred from the soluble kinase fold and membrane-targeting PH domain. The absence of transmembrane segments and the soluble AGC architecture indicate a cytosolic enzyme that transiently associates with membranes. Therefore, a cytoplasmic localization is expected, aligning with GO:0005737 cytoplasm and the observed soluble nature of the architecture.

Mechanistically, the PH module first concentrates the kinase at PI(3,4,5)P3/PI(3,4)P2-rich membrane microdomains. There, activation-loop and hydrophobic-motif phosphorylation switch the AGC tail into an active state, enabling phosphorylation of cytosolic and peripheral-membrane substrates that govern metabolism and survival. Likely interaction partners follow from this model: upstream activators and adaptors that dock via PH or scaffolds; 14-3-3 proteins that stabilize phosphorylated states; and membrane-proximal substrates that relay PI3K signals. Together these interactions coordinate cytoplasmic signaling assemblies that couple lipid cues to phosphorylation cascades.

Functional Summary

A cytoplasmic AGC-type serine/threonine kinase that uses an N‑terminal pleckstrin‑homology module to dock onto phosphoinositide-enriched membranes and become activated. Once recruited and switched into its active conformation through its regulatory C‑terminal tail, it phosphorylates downstream targets to propagate phosphatidylinositol-driven signaling, thereby coordinating intracellular signaling, cell growth and survival programs from a soluble cytoplasmic hub.

UniProt Summary

May play an important role in cellular processes involving the cytoplasmic action of the second messenger ins phosphatidylinositol 3,4,5-trisphosphate.

InterPro Domains

  • PH-like domain superfamily (IPR011993, homologous_superfamily) — residues 1-123
  • Protein Kinase B, pleckstrin homology domain (IPR039026, domain) — residues 4-111
  • Pleckstrin homology domain (IPR001849, domain) — residues 5-110
  • Protein kinase B alpha, catalytic domain (IPR034676, domain) — residues 124-479
  • Protein kinase-like domain superfamily (IPR011009, homologous_superfamily) — residues 145-459
  • Protein kinase domain (IPR000719, domain) — residues 150-408
  • Protein kinase, ATP binding site (IPR017441, binding_site) — residues 156-189
  • Serine/threonine-protein kinase, active site (IPR008271, active_site) — residues 270-282
  • AGC-kinase, C-terminal (IPR000961, domain) — residues 409-480
  • Protein kinase, C-terminal (IPR017892, domain) — residues 429-474

Deep Research Bioreason Rl

(Akt1-deep-research-bioreason-rl.md)

BioReason Chat Export

Exported on March 22, 2026 at 03:11 AM

Organism: Mus musculus

Sequence:

MNDVAIVKEGWLHKRGEYIKTWRPRYFLLKNDGTFIGYKERPQDVDQRESPLNNFSVAQCQLMKTERPRPNTFIIRCLQWTTVIERTFHVETPEEREEWATAIQTVADGLKRQEEETMDFRSGSPSDNSGAEEMEVSLAKPKHRVTMNEFEYLKLLGKGTFGKVILVKEKATGRYYAMKILKKEVIVAKDEVAHTLTENRVLQNSRHPFLTALKYSFQTHDRLCFVMEYANGGELFFHLSRERVFSEDRARFYGAEIVSALDYLHSEKNVVYRDLKLENLMLDKDGHIKITDFGLCKEGIKDGATMKTFCGTPEYLAPEVLEDNDYGRAVDWWGLGVVMYEMMCGRLPFYNQDHEKLFELILMEEIRFPRTLGPEAKSLLSGLLKKDPTQRLGGGSEDAKEIMQHRFFANIVWQDVYEKKLSPPFKPQVTSETDTRYFDEEFTAQMITITPPDQDDSMECVDSERRPHFPQFSYSASGTA

Thinking Trace

I begin with the InterPro architecture. The N-terminus (residues 1–123) carries IPR011993 (PH-like domain superfamily), within which three pleckstrin-homology annotations converge: IPR039026 (Protein Kinase B, pleckstrin homology domain, residues 4–111), IPR001849 (Pleckstrin homology domain, residues 5–110), and the AKT/kinase B PH signature IPR034676 (Protein kinase B alpha, catalytic domain, residues 124–479). This tandem clearly establishes a canonical PH module that binds phosphoinositide headgroups and thereby recruits the kinase to membrane-proximal signaling sites. Immediately downstream lies the bilobal catalytic core: IPR034676 overlaps and transitions into the kinase region marked by IPR011009 (Protein kinase-like domain superfamily, residues 145–459) and IPR000719 (Protein kinase domain, residues 150–408). The active chemistry is specified by IPR017441 (Protein kinase, ATP binding site, residues 156–189) and IPR008271 (Serine/threonine-protein kinase, active site, residues 270–282), which together define an ATP-dependent serine/threonine phosphotransferase mechanism. The C-terminus is characteristic of AGC kinases: IPR000961 (AGC-kinase, C-terminal, residues 409–480) and IPR017892 (Protein kinase, C-terminal, residues 429–474) encode the regulatory tail that stabilizes the active conformation and integrates activation-loop and hydrophobic motif phosphorylation.

This ordered layout—membrane-targeting PH domain followed by an AGC-type catalytic core and regulatory tail—causes ATP-dependent serine/threonine kinase activity. The PH domain dictates lipid-dependent localization and substrate selection, while the kinase core and AGC tail execute phosphorylation of protein substrates; together these features support GO:0004674 protein serine/threonine kinase activity and the broader GO:0004672 protein kinase activity. The presence of a high-affinity ATP-binding pocket (residues 156–189) and an AGC activation segment (residues 270–282) ensures robust catalytic function.

From this molecular function, the biological process follows. PH-directed recruitment to phosphoinositide-enriched membranes positions the enzyme downstream of PI3K signaling. There, it phosphorylates targets that regulate metabolism, growth, and survival. This places the protein squarely in phosphatidylinositol-mediated signaling and explains its role in broader intracellular signaling cascades. Thus, the kinase architecture and membrane targeting logically drive GO:0006468 protein phosphorylation and implicate processes such as PI3K signaling and its downstream pathways.

The cellular component is inferred from the soluble kinase fold and membrane-targeting PH domain. The absence of transmembrane segments and the soluble AGC architecture indicate a cytosolic enzyme that transiently associates with membranes. Therefore, a cytoplasmic localization is expected, aligning with GO:0005737 cytoplasm and the observed soluble nature of the architecture.

Mechanistically, the PH module first concentrates the kinase at PI(3,4,5)P3/PI(3,4)P2-rich membrane microdomains. There, activation-loop and hydrophobic-motif phosphorylation switch the AGC tail into an active state, enabling phosphorylation of cytosolic and peripheral-membrane substrates that govern metabolism and survival. Likely interaction partners follow from this model: upstream activators and adaptors that dock via PH or scaffolds; 14-3-3 proteins that stabilize phosphorylated states; and membrane-proximal substrates that relay PI3K signals. Together these interactions coordinate cytoplasmic signaling assemblies that couple lipid cues to phosphorylation cascades.

Functional Summary

A cytoplasmic AGC-type serine/threonine kinase that uses an N‑terminal pleckstrin‑homology module to dock onto phosphoinositide-enriched membranes and become activated. Once recruited and switched into its active conformation through its regulatory C‑terminal tail, it phosphorylates downstream targets to propagate phosphatidylinositol-driven signaling, thereby coordinating intracellular signaling, cell growth and survival programs from a soluble cytoplasmic hub.

UniProt Summary

May play an important role in cellular processes involving the cytoplasmic action of the second messenger ins phosphatidylinositol 3,4,5-trisphosphate.

Generated by BioReason

Bioreason Rl Review

(Akt1-bioreason-rl-review.md)

BioReason-Pro RL Review: Akt1 (mouse)

Source: Akt1-deep-research-bioreason-rl.md

  • Correctness: 5/5
  • Completeness: 4/5

Functional Summary Review

The BioReason functional summary is accurate and well-constructed:

A cytoplasmic AGC-type serine/threonine kinase that uses an N-terminal pleckstrin-homology module to dock onto phosphoinositide-enriched membranes and become activated. Once recruited and switched into its active conformation through its regulatory C-terminal tail, it phosphorylates downstream targets to propagate phosphatidylinositol-driven signaling, thereby coordinating intracellular signaling, cell growth and survival programs from a soluble cytoplasmic hub.

This correctly identifies all major features: (1) the AGC-type serine/threonine kinase identity, (2) the PH domain-mediated membrane recruitment by PI(3,4,5)P3/PI(3,4)P2, (3) the C-terminal regulatory tail characteristic of AGC kinases, and (4) the downstream roles in growth and survival. The description aligns well with GO:0004674 (protein serine/threonine kinase activity), GO:0005737 (cytoplasm), GO:0006468 (protein phosphorylation), and GO:0035556 (intracellular signal transduction) -- all core curated annotations for Akt1.

The summary accurately places Akt1 downstream of PI3K signaling and describes the PH-domain-driven membrane translocation mechanism, which is the canonical activation mode. The mention of "cell growth and survival programs" is appropriate and well-supported.

The only minor gap is that specific well-established downstream targets and pathways (e.g., mTOR signaling, GSK3 phosphorylation, glucose homeostasis, anti-apoptotic signaling via BAD phosphorylation) are not mentioned, though this is understandable for a domain-architecture-driven summary.

Comparison with interpro2go:

No GO_REF:0000002 annotations exist for mouse Akt1 in the curated review; IEA annotations come from UniRule (GO_REF:0000120) instead. The BioReason predictions include the same core terms (GO:0004674, GO:0004672, GO:0006468, GO:0005737) that UniRule and IBA annotations provide. BioReason adds narrative value by explaining the mechanistic logic connecting the PH domain to membrane recruitment and kinase activation, which goes beyond what interpro2go-style mappings provide. The functional summary is not merely recapitulating automated annotations but synthesizing them into a coherent mechanistic narrative.

Notes on thinking trace

The thinking trace methodically walks through the InterPro domain architecture from N-terminus to C-terminus, correctly interpreting the PH domain, kinase core, and AGC tail. The reasoning from domain architecture to molecular function to biological process to cellular component is logical and well-ordered. The trace appropriately identifies the lipid-binding specificity of the PH domain and the AGC-family activation mechanism.

references:
- id: GO_REF:0000008
  title: Gene Ontology annotation by the MGI curatorial staff, curated orthology
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000024
  title: Manual transfer of experimentally-verified manual GO annotation data to orthologs by curator judgment of sequence
    similarity
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000033
  title: Annotation inferences using phylogenetic trees
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000044
  title: Gene Ontology annotation based on UniProtKB/Swiss-Prot Subcellular Location vocabulary mapping, accompanied by conservative
    changes to GO terms applied by UniProt
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000096
  title: Automated transfer of experimentally-verified manual GO annotation data to mouse-rat orthologs
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000107
  title: Automatic transfer of experimentally verified manual GO annotation data to orthologs using Ensembl Compara
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000108
  title: Automatic assignment of GO terms using logical inference, based on on inter-ontology links
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000117
  title: Electronic Gene Ontology annotations created by ARBA machine learning models
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000119
  title: Automated transfer of experimentally-verified manual GO annotation data to mouse-human orthologs
  findings: []
- id: GO_REF:0000120
  title: Combined Automated Annotation using Multiple IEA Methods
  findings: []
